19.notes:
Leviticus - Chapter 19
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God calls Israel to holiness in everyday life — love your neighbor as yourself, care for the poor, deal honestly, and revere God in all things.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Leviticus is the third book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Vayikra"
— "And He called." God speaks from the newly completed Tabernacle, giving
Israel instructions for worship, sacrifice, and holy living.

Historical Context: Chapter 19 is the heart of the Holiness Code and one
of the most remarkable chapters in the entire Bible. It opens with the
foundational command: "Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy"
(v.2). What follows is a sweeping collection of laws that touch every area
of life — family, worship, economics, agriculture, justice, speech,
relationships, and love. The chapter is addressed to "all the congregation"
(v.2) — not just priests, not just leaders, but every Israelite. Holiness
is not reserved for a spiritual elite; it is the calling of the entire
community. The chapter contains the second greatest commandment: "thou
shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" (v.18) — quoted by Jesus as the
summary of the law's second table (Matthew 22:39; Mark 12:31). Many of
the Ten Commandments are echoed here (honor parents, keep Sabbath, no
idols, no stealing, no lying, no false witness), but they are expanded
and applied to practical daily life. The refrain "I am the LORD" (or "I
am the LORD your God") appears sixteen times — grounding every command in
God's identity. Holiness is not abstract — it is concrete, practical, and
all-encompassing.

The Foundation: Be Holy (vv.1-4): God speaks to Moses for "all the
congregation" (v.1-2). "Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am
holy" (v.2) — the thesis statement of the entire Holiness Code. Holiness
is not optional; it is the nature of God and therefore the calling of His
people. Immediately practical: "Ye shall fear every man his mother, and
his father" (v.3) — honor parents (note: mother listed first, unusual).
"Keep my sabbaths" (v.3). "Turn ye not unto idols, nor make to yourselves
molten gods" (v.4). Family, worship, and loyalty to God — the foundations.

Sacrifice and Worship (vv.5-8): Peace offerings must be eaten within two
days; anything remaining on the third day must be burned (vv.5-6). Eating
it on the third day is "abominable" — the offering is profaned and the
person bears iniquity and is cut off (vv.7-8). Worship must follow God's
instructions precisely.

Economic Justice and Compassion (vv.9-10): Gleaning laws — do not harvest
to the very edges of your field; do not pick up what falls (v.9). Do not
strip your vineyard bare or gather fallen grapes (v.10). Leave them "for
the poor and stranger" (v.10). This is institutionalized compassion —
built into the economic system. The story of Ruth depends on this law
(Ruth 2:2-3).

The Ethical Commands (vv.11-18): A rapid-fire series of moral commands:
No stealing, no lying, no false dealing (v.11). No false swearing by God's
name (v.12). No oppression or robbery; pay wages on time (v.13). No
cursing the deaf or tripping the blind (v.14) — protecting the vulnerable
who cannot defend themselves. No injustice in court — neither favoring the
poor nor deferring to the rich (v.15). No slander; no standing against
your neighbor's life (v.16). No hatred in the heart; rebuke your neighbor
openly rather than harboring sin (v.17). No vengeance or grudge-bearing
(v.18). THE CLIMAX: "thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the
LORD" (v.18). Love is the fulfillment of all these commands.

Laws of Separation (v.19): "Ye shall keep my statutes" — then three
prohibitions: no crossbreeding cattle, no sowing mixed seed, no garments
of mixed fabric (linen and wool). These laws teach the principle of
distinction — God separates, and His people maintain distinctions. They
are object lessons in holiness (separation/distinction).

Sexual Sin and Its Remedy (vv.20-22): A man who lies with a betrothed
slave woman — "she shall be scourged" (KJV) / "a distinction shall be
made" (ESV) (v.20). They are not put to death because she was not free
(v.20). He brings a guilt/trespass offering — a ram (v.21). The priest
makes atonement and he is forgiven (v.22). Even in cases of sexual sin,
there is a path to forgiveness through sacrifice.

Agricultural Laws (vv.23-25): Fruit trees in the land — the fruit is
"uncircumcised" (forbidden) for three years (v.23). In the fourth year,
all fruit is holy to the LORD (v.24). In the fifth year, they may eat it
(v.25). Patience, consecration, then enjoyment. First fruits belong to God.

Miscellaneous Holiness Laws (vv.26-31): No eating with blood (v.26). No
divination or fortune-telling (v.26). No rounding the corners of the head
or marring the beard (v.27) — likely prohibiting pagan mourning customs.
No cutting the body for the dead or tattoo marks (v.28). Do not prostitute
your daughter (v.29). Keep Sabbaths and reverence the sanctuary (v.30).
Do not consult mediums or necromancers (v.31).

Honor, Love, and Justice (vv.32-37): Rise before the elderly and honor
them (v.32). Do not wrong the stranger — love him as yourself, "for ye
were strangers in the land of Egypt" (vv.33-34). No dishonesty in
measurements — just balances, just weights, just measures (vv.35-36).
"I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt" (v.36)
— the exodus is the motivation for justice. Final summary: "observe all my
statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: I am the LORD" (v.37).

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/leviticus-19/
  Guzik calls this chapter "a remarkable collection of laws" that shows
  holiness touching every area of life. He emphasizes that "love your
  neighbor as yourself" is not a New Testament invention but an Old
  Testament command — Jesus was quoting Moses. He notes the balance of
  justice in v.15: neither favoring the poor nor the rich.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Love Thy Neighbour"
  "Love thy neighbour as thyself — this is not a suggestion, it is a
  COMMAND. And it is not a new command — it is as old as Leviticus. Our
  Lord did not invent it; He quoted it. Moses wrote it by the Spirit of
  God a thousand years before Christ walked the earth. And what does it
  mean? It means: treat your neighbour's interests as you treat your own.
  His reputation — guard it as you guard yours. His property — respect it
  as you respect yours. His feelings — consider them as you consider yours.
  His needs — attend to them as you attend to yours. This is not natural
  to fallen man. It requires a new heart, a new nature, the very Spirit
  of God within. But it is the standard — and Christ both commands it and
  enables it."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy" / ESV: "You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" — Hebrew "Q'doshim tih'yu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem." Identical in both. "Q'doshim" (holy ones) — plural, addressed to all. "Qadosh" (holy) — set apart, distinct, other. God's holiness is the ground and model of Israel's holiness.
v.3              | KJV         | "Ye shall fear every man his mother, and his father" / ESV: "Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father" — Hebrew "ish immo v'aviv tira'u" — each person his mother and his father you shall fear/revere. "Yare" = fear/reverence (same word used for fearing God). Mother listed FIRST — unusual in patriarchal context. Perhaps because reverence for the mother is more easily neglected.
v.14             | KJV         | "put a stumblingblock before the blind" / ESV: "put a stumbling block before the blind" — Hebrew "lifnei iver lo titen mikhshol." Literally: before a blind person do not place an obstacle. Taken literally AND metaphorically in Jewish tradition: do not mislead the ignorant, do not give bad advice to the naive, do not enable sin in the vulnerable.
v.17             | KJV         | "thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbour, and not suffer sin upon him" / ESV: "you shall reason frankly with your neighbor, lest you incur sin because of him" — Hebrew "hokhe'ach tokhiach et amitekha v'lo tissa alav chet." KJV: rebuke him so that sin does not remain on him. ESV: reason with him lest YOU bear sin because of him. The Hebrew allows both: failure to rebuke makes you complicit in his sin. Honest confrontation is an act of love.
v.18             | KJV         | "love thy neighbour as thyself" / ESV: "love your neighbor as yourself" — Hebrew "v'ahavta l're'akha kamokha." "Ahav" = love (the same word used in "love the LORD your God" — Deuteronomy 6:5). "Re'a" = neighbor, companion, fellow. "Kamokha" = as yourself. Jesus identifies this as the second greatest commandment (Matthew 22:39).
v.19             | KJV         | "a garment mingled of linen and woollen" / ESV: "a garment of cloth made of two kinds of material" — Hebrew "sha'atnez" — a technical term for mixed fabric (specifically linen and wool). KJV names the specific fabrics; ESV generalizes. The prohibition is specifically linen + wool (Deuteronomy 22:11). Interestingly, the priestly garments DID combine linen and wool — what is forbidden to the common person is prescribed for the priest.
v.26             | KJV         | "neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times" / ESV: "You shall not interpret omens or tell fortunes" — Hebrew "lo t'nachashu v'lo t'onenu." KJV's "observe times" = divination by times/seasons (astrology). ESV's "tell fortunes" captures the meaning. Both forms of divination are prohibited.
v.27             | KJV         | "Ye shall not round the corners of your heads" / ESV: "You shall not round off the hair on your temples" — Hebrew "lo takifu pe'at rosh'khem." Refers to cutting the hair in a specific pattern associated with pagan mourning or worship customs. Not a prohibition of haircuts generally, but of pagan-associated styles.
v.28             | KJV         | "nor print any marks upon you" / ESV: "or tattoo yourselves" — Hebrew "u-kh'tovet qa'aqa lo tit'nu ba-khem" — and a writing/ inscription of incision you shall not put on yourselves. KJV's "print marks" is literal; ESV's "tattoo" uses the modern term. The context is pagan mourning rituals — marking the body for the dead.
v.35             | KJV         | "in meteyard, in weight, or in measure" / ESV: "in measures of length or weight or quantity" — Hebrew "ba-middah ba-mishqal u-va-m'surah." KJV's "meteyard" is archaic for a measuring rod (length). ESV modernizes. All forms of measurement must be honest.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Be holy" (Q'doshim tih'yu) | The imperative that governs the entire chapter. "Qadosh" means set apart, distinct, other. To be holy is to be like God — distinct from the world, set apart for His purposes. Not merely moral purity but total consecration. | 
Love thy neighbour as thyself | The second greatest commandment (Matthew 22:39). Not a feeling but an action: treat others' interests as you treat your own. The standard is not perfection but self-interest redirected outward. "As yourself" = with the same concern, attention, and care you naturally give to your own needs. | 
Gleaning          | The practice of leaving the edges of fields unharvested and not picking up dropped grain or fallen fruit, so that the poor and foreigners may gather food. An institutionalized form of welfare that preserves the dignity of the poor (they work for their food) while requiring generosity from the landowner. | 
I am the LORD     | The refrain that punctuates the chapter (16 times). Functions as both authority ("I command this") and motivation ("because of who I am"). God's identity is the ground of all ethics. Every command flows from His character. | 
Stumbling block (Mikhshol) | An obstacle placed in someone's path. Used literally (tripping the blind) and metaphorically (causing someone to sin). The principle: do not exploit another's weakness or ignorance for your advantage. | 
Mixed kinds (Kilayim) | The prohibition of mixing species (cattle), seeds (agriculture), and fabrics (clothing). Teaches the principle of distinction — God creates categories and His people respect them. A living parable of holiness (separation/distinction). | 
Fear thy God      | Hebrew "v'yareta me-Elohekha" — and you shall fear your God. Appears after commands that could be violated secretly (cursing the deaf, misleading the blind, unjust judgment). When no human witness can hold you accountable, remember: God sees. Fear HIM. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Be ye holy: for I the LORD your God am holy" (v.2). Holiness is not
   optional for believers — it is the family resemblance. God's children
   bear His character. And holiness is not abstract — this chapter shows it
   is intensely practical: how you treat your parents, your employees, the
   disabled, the poor, the foreigner, your neighbor. Where is your holiness
   merely theoretical rather than practical?

2. "Leave them for the poor and stranger" (v.10). God builds compassion
   into the economic system. Generosity is not merely voluntary charity —
   it is structural justice. Are you using all your resources for yourself,
   or leaving "edges" for those in need?

3. "Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the
   blind" (v.14). Do not exploit those who cannot defend themselves or
   detect your exploitation. God sees what the deaf cannot hear and what
   the blind cannot see. "Fear thy God" — He is the witness when no one
   else is watching.

4. "Love thy neighbour as thyself" (v.18). The standard is not "love your
   neighbor as much as you can manage" but "as yourself." With the same
   urgency, the same concern, the same investment you naturally give to
   your own interests. This is impossible without the Spirit of God. But it
   is the standard — and it is the fulfillment of the entire law (Romans
   13:9; Galatians 5:14).

5. "The stranger that dwelleth with you shall be unto you as one born among
   you" (v.34). Love is not limited to your own people, your own tribe,
   your own kind. The foreigner is to be loved "as yourself" — the same
   standard as the neighbor. "For ye were strangers in the land of Egypt" —
   remember where you came from. You were once the outsider.

20.ESV:
1. The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
2. “Say to the people of Israel, Any one of the people of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death. The people of the land shall stone him with stones.
3. I myself will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, to make my sanctuary unclean and to profane my holy name.
4. And if the people of the land do at all close their eyes to that man when he gives one of his children to Molech, and do not put him to death,
5. then I will set my face against that man and against his clan and will cut them off from among their people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech.
6. “If a person turns to mediums and necromancers, whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off from among his people.
7. Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am the LORD your God.
8. Keep my statutes and do them; I am the LORD who sanctifies you.
9. For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death; he has cursed his father or his mother; his blood is upon him.
10. “If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.
11. If a man lies with his father’s wife, he has uncovered his father’s nakedness; both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.
12. If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death; they have committed perversion; their blood is upon them.
13. If a man lies with a male as with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.
14. If a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity; he and they shall be burned with fire, that there may be no depravity among you.
15. If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death, and you shall kill the animal.
16. If a woman approaches any animal and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.
17. “If a man takes his sister, a daughter of his father or a daughter of his mother, and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace, and they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has uncovered his sister’s nakedness, and he shall bear his iniquity.
18. If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual period and uncovers her nakedness, he has made naked her fountain, and she has uncovered the fountain of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people.
19. You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother’s sister or of your father’s sister, for that is to make naked one’s relative; they shall bear their iniquity.
20. If a man lies with his uncle’s wife, he has uncovered his uncle’s nakedness; they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless.
21. If a man takes his brother’s wife, it is impurity. He has uncovered his brother’s nakedness; they shall be childless.
22. “You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out.
23. And you shall not walk in the customs of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and therefore I detested them.
24. But I have said to you, ‘You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess, a land flowing with milk and honey.’ I am the LORD your God, who has separated you from the peoples.
25. You shall therefore separate the clean beast from the unclean, and the unclean bird from the clean. You shall not make yourselves detestable by beast or by bird or by anything with which the ground crawls, which I have set apart for you to hold unclean.
26. You shall be holy to me, for I the LORD am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.
27. “A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer shall surely be put to death. They shall be stoned with stones; their blood shall be upon them.”

20.NET:
1. The Lord spoke to Moses:
2. “You are to say to the Israelites, ‘Any man from the Israelites (or any of the resident foreigners who live in Israel) who gives any of his children to Molech must be put to death; the people of the land must pelt him with stones.
3. I myself will set my face against that man and cut him off from the midst of his people, because he has given some of his children to Molech and thereby defiled my sanctuary and profaned my holy name.
4. If, however, the people of the land shut their eyes to that man when he gives some of his children to Molech so that they do not put him to death,
5. I myself will set my face against that man and his clan. I will cut off from the midst of the people both him and all who follow after him in spiritual prostitution, committing prostitution by worshiping Molech.
6. “‘The person who turns to the spirits of the dead and familiar spirits to commit prostitution by going after them, I will set my face against that person and cut him off from the midst of his people.
7. “‘You must sanctify yourselves and be holy, because I am the Lord your God.
8. You must be sure to obey my statutes. I am the Lord who sanctifies you.
9. “‘If anyone curses his father or mother, he must be put to death. He has cursed his father or mother; his blood guilt is on himself.
10. If a man commits adultery with his neighbor’s wife, both the adulterer and the adulteress must be put to death.
11. If a man goes to bed with his father’s wife, he has exposed his father’s nakedness. Both of them must be put to death; their blood guilt is on themselves.
12. If a man goes to bed with his daughter-in-law, both of them must be put to death. They have committed perversion; their blood guilt is on themselves.
13. If a man goes to bed with a male as one goes to bed with a woman, the two of them have committed an abomination. They must be put to death; their blood guilt is on themselves.
14. If a man has marital relations with both a woman and her mother, it is lewdness. Both he and they must be burned to death, so there is no lewdness in your midst.
15. If a man has sexual relations with any animal, he must be put to death, and you must kill the animal.
16. If a woman approaches any animal to copulate with it, you must kill the woman, and the animal must be put to death; their blood guilt is on themselves.
17. “‘If a man has marital relations with his sister, whether the daughter of his father or of his mother, so that he sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace. They must be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has exposed his sister’s nakedness; he will bear his punishment for iniquity.
18. If a man goes to bed with a menstruating woman and uncovers her nakedness, he has laid bare her fountain of blood, and she has exposed the fountain of her blood, so both of them must be cut off from the midst of their people.
19. You must not expose the nakedness of your mother’s sister or your father’s sister, for such a person has exposed his own close relative. They must bear their punishment for iniquity.
20. If a man goes to bed with his aunt, he has exposed his uncle’s nakedness; they must bear responsibility for their sin, they will die childless.
21. If a man has marital relations with his brother’s wife, it is indecency. He has exposed his brother’s nakedness; they will be childless.
22. “‘You must be sure to obey all my statutes and regulations, so that the land to which I am about to bring you to take up residence does not vomit you out.
23. You must not walk in the statutes of the nations which I am about to drive out before you, because they have done all these things and I am filled with disgust against them.
24. So I have said to you: You yourselves will possess their land and I myself will give it to you for a possession, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the Lord your God who has set you apart from the other peoples.
25. Therefore you must distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean, and you must not make yourselves detestable by means of an animal or bird or anything that creeps on the ground—creatures I have distinguished for you as unclean.
26. You must be holy to me because I, the Lord, am holy, and I have set you apart from the other peoples to be mine.
27. “‘A man or woman who has in them a spirit of the dead or a familiar spirit must be put to death. They must pelt them with stones; their blood guilt is on themselves.’”
20.WEB:
1. Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
2. “Moreover, you shall tell the children of Israel, ‘Anyone of the children of Israel, or of the strangers who live as foreigners in Israel, who gives any of his offspring to Molech; he shall surely be put to death. The people of the land shall stone him with stones.
3. I also will set my face against that person, and will cut him off from among his people because he has given of his offspring to Molech, to defile my sanctuary, and to profane my holy name.
4. If the people of the land all hide their eyes from that person, when he gives of his offspring to Molech, and don’t put him to death;
5. then I will set my face against that man, and against his family, and will cut him off, and all who play the prostitute after him, to play the prostitute with Molech, from among their people.
6. “‘The person that turns to those who are mediums, and to the wizards, to play the prostitute after them, I will even set my face against that person, and will cut him off from among his people.
7. “‘Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be holy; for I am Yahweh your God.
8. You shall keep my statutes, and do them. I am Yahweh who sanctifies you.
9. “‘For everyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death: he has cursed his father or his mother; his blood shall be upon him.
10. “‘The man who commits adultery with another man’s wife, even he who commits adultery with his neighbor’s wife, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.
11. “‘The man who lies with his father’s wife has uncovered his father’s nakedness: both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
12. “‘If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death: they have committed a perversion; their blood shall be upon them.
13. “‘If a man lies with a male, as with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination: they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
14. “‘If a man takes a wife and her mother, it is wickedness: they shall be burned with fire, both he and they; that there may be no wickedness among you.
15. “‘If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death; and you shall kill the animal.
16. “‘If a woman approaches any animal, and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal. They shall surely be put to death. Their blood shall be upon them.
17. “‘If a man takes his sister, his father’s daughter, or his mother’s daughter, and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness; it is a shameful thing. They shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has uncovered his sister’s nakedness. He shall bear his iniquity.
18. “‘If a man lies with a woman having her monthly period, and uncovers her nakedness; he has made naked her fountain, and she has uncovered the fountain of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people.
19. “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother’s sister, nor of your father’s sister; for he has made his close relative naked. They shall bear their iniquity.
20. If a man lies with his uncle’s wife, he has uncovered his uncle’s nakedness. They shall bear their sin. They shall die childless.
21. “‘If a man takes his brother’s wife, it is an impurity. He has uncovered his brother’s nakedness. They shall be childless.
22. “‘You shall therefore keep all my statutes, and all my ordinances, and do them; that the land, where I am bringing you to dwell, may not vomit you out.
23. You shall not walk in the customs of the nation, which I am casting out before you: for they did all these things, and therefore I abhorred them.
24. But I have said to you, “You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess it, a land flowing with milk and honey.” I am Yahweh your God, who has separated you from the peoples.
25. “‘You shall therefore make a distinction between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean fowl and the clean: and you shall not make yourselves abominable by animal, or by bird, or by anything with which the ground teems, which I have separated from you as unclean for you.
26. You shall be holy to me; for I, Yahweh, am holy, and have set you apart from the peoples, that you should be mine.
27. “‘A man or a woman that is a medium, or is a wizard, shall surely be put to death: they shall stone them with stones. Their blood shall be upon them.’”
20.notes:
Leviticus - Chapter 20
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God prescribes penalties for idolatry, occult practices, and sexual sins — holiness has consequences, and God separates His people from wickedness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Leviticus is the third book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Vayikra"
— "And He called." God speaks from the newly completed Tabernacle, giving
Israel instructions for worship, sacrifice, and holy living.

Historical Context: Chapter 20 is the companion to chapter 18. Where
chapter 18 lists the prohibited sexual acts, chapter 20 prescribes the
PENALTIES for those acts. The two chapters form a pair: prohibition (ch.18)
and punishment (ch.20), with chapter 19 (practical holiness) sandwiched
between them. The chapter also addresses penalties for Molech worship
(vv.1-5) and consulting mediums (v.6), which were mentioned in chapter 19.
The structure is deliberate: the most severe penalties (death) come first,
followed by lesser penalties ("cut off," childlessness). The chapter opens
and closes with calls to holiness (vv.7-8, 26) — the penalties are not
arbitrary cruelty but the necessary enforcement of holiness in a covenant
community. God is forming a nation that will be distinct from all others:
"I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye
should be mine" (v.26). The severity of the penalties reflects the
seriousness of the sins — these are not minor infractions but acts that
destroy families, defile the land, and profane God's name.

Molech Worship — Death Penalty (vv.1-5): Anyone who gives his children to
Molech "shall surely be put to death" — stoned by the people (v.2). God
Himself will "set my face against that man" and cut him off (v.3). The
reasons: it defiles God's sanctuary and profanes His holy name (v.3). If
the community ignores it ("hide their eyes"), God will act against the man,
his family, and all who follow him in this idolatry (vv.4-5). Communal
responsibility: tolerating evil makes the community complicit.

Mediums and Necromancers (v.6): Anyone who turns to mediums or spiritists
— God will "set my face against that soul" and cut him off. Seeking
guidance from the dead or from spirits is spiritual adultery ("whoring
after them").

The Call to Holiness (vv.7-8): "Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be ye
holy: for I am the LORD your God" (v.7). "Ye shall keep my statutes, and
do them: I am the LORD which sanctify you" (v.8). God both commands
holiness AND provides it — "I am the LORD which sanctify you." Holiness
is both human responsibility and divine gift.

Cursing Parents — Death (v.9): "Every one that curseth his father or his
mother shall be surely put to death" (v.9). "His blood shall be upon him"
— he bears responsibility for his own death. The family is the foundation
of society; attacking it at its root (dishonoring parents) is a capital
offense.

Adultery — Death (v.10): Both adulterer and adulteress "shall surely be
put to death." The marriage covenant is sacred; its violation is treated
with the same severity as murder.

Incest and Sexual Perversion — Death (vv.11-16): Father's wife (v.11) —
death. Daughter-in-law (v.12) — death; "they have wrought confusion."
Homosexuality (v.13) — death; "they have committed an abomination." A man
taking a woman and her mother (v.14) — burned with fire; "it is
wickedness." Bestiality — man (v.15) or woman (v.16) — death for both
person and animal.

Lesser Penalties — Cut Off / Childlessness (vv.17-21): Sister (v.17) —
"cut off in the sight of their people." During menstruation (v.18) — both
"cut off from among their people." Mother's or father's sister (v.19) —
"they shall bear their iniquity." Uncle's wife (v.20) — "they shall die
childless." Brother's wife (v.21) — "they shall be childless." The
graduated penalties show degrees of severity — but all are serious.

The Closing Exhortation (vv.22-27): "Keep all my statutes... that the
land... spue you not out" (v.22). Do not walk in the customs of the
nations being driven out — "I abhorred them" (v.23). "I have said unto
you, Ye shall inherit their land" — a land flowing with milk and honey
(v.24). "I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other
people" (v.24). Distinguish clean from unclean (v.25). THE CLIMAX: "And
ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you
from other people, that ye should be mine" (v.26). Mediums and
necromancers — stoned to death (v.27). The chapter ends where it began:
with the death penalty for spiritual rebellion.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | 2 Kings     | 23      | 10                 | Josiah destroys Topheth to stop Molech worship.
v.2              | Jeremiah    | 7       | 31                 | "They have built the high places of Tophet... to burn their sons and their daughters in the fire."
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 28      | 7-19               | Saul consults the medium at Endor — and is condemned for it (1 Chronicles 10:13-14).
v.6              | Deuteronomy | 18      | 10-12              | All forms of divination are abomination.
v.7              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 15-16              | "Be holy in all your conduct." (ESV)
v.8              | 1 Thessalonians | 5       | 23-24              | "May the God of peace himself sanctify you completely... He who calls you is faithful; he will surely do it." (ESV)
v.9              | Exodus      | 20      | 12                 | "Honor your father and your mother." (ESV)
v.9              | Matthew     | 15      | 4                  | Jesus quotes: "Whoever reviles father or mother must surely die." (ESV)
v.9              | Mark        | 7       | 10                 | Jesus cites this law against the Pharisees' "Corban" tradition.
v.10             | John        | 8       | 4-5                | The Pharisees cite this law regarding the woman caught in adultery.
v.10             | Hebrews     | 13      | 4                  | "God will judge the sexually immoral and adulterous." (ESV)
v.13             | Romans      | 1       | 26-27              | Homosexual practice described as "against nature."
v.13             | 1 Corinthians | 6       | 9-11               | "Such were some of you. But you were washed." (ESV)
v.22             | Leviticus   | 18      | 28                 | "lest the land vomit you out." (ESV)
v.22             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 63                 | The curse of exile for disobedience.
v.24             | Exodus      | 3       | 8                  | "A land flowing with milk and honey."
v.26             | 1 Peter     | 2       | 9                  | "a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession." (ESV)
v.26             | Titus       | 2       | 14                 | Christ gave Himself "to redeem us... and to purify for himself a people for his own possession." (ESV)
v.27             | Exodus      | 22      | 18                 | "You shall not permit a sorceress to live." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/leviticus-20/
  Guzik notes the relationship between chapters 18 and 20 (prohibition
  and penalty) and emphasizes that the severity of penalties reflects God's
  view of these sins, not human cruelty. He highlights v.26 as the key:
  separation is for the purpose of BELONGING — "that ye should be mine."

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "A Peculiar People"
  "God says: I have SEVERED you from other people, that ye should be MINE.
  There is the whole of it — separation FOR possession. God does not
  separate His people merely to make them different; He separates them to
  make them HIS. You are not merely called out of the world; you are called
  INTO God's own family. The holiness He demands is not cold isolation but
  warm belonging. 'That ye should be mine' — this is the sweetest word in
  all of Leviticus. You are His peculiar treasure, His special possession,
  His beloved people. And because you are His, you must be holy — for He
  is holy. A king's child bears the king's character."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "giveth any of his seed unto Molech" / ESV: "gives any of his children to Molech" — Hebrew "yitten mi-zar'o la-Molekh" — gives from his seed to Molech. KJV's "seed" is literal; ESV's "children" is interpretive but correct. "Seed" (zera) here means offspring.
v.3              | KJV         | "to defile my sanctuary" / ESV: "to make my sanctuary unclean" — Hebrew "l'tamme et miqdashi." Both convey the same meaning. Child sacrifice defiles God's sanctuary — even though it occurs outside the sanctuary. Sin committed in the community pollutes the place where God dwells.
v.6              | KJV         | "familiar spirits... wizards" / ESV: "mediums and necromancers" — Hebrew "ha-ovot v'ha-yid'onim." "Ov" = a spirit of the dead, or one who channels such spirits (a medium). "Yid'oni" = a knowing one, a spiritist (one who claims supernatural knowledge). KJV's "familiar spirits" refers to the spirit that is "familiar" (known/attached) to the medium. ESV uses modern terminology.
v.9              | KJV         | "curseth his father or his mother" / ESV: "curses his father or his mother" — Hebrew "y'qallel et aviv v'et immo." "Qalal" = to make light of, to curse, to treat with contempt. The opposite of "kavad" (to honor/make heavy). Not merely profanity but fundamental disrespect — treating parents as worthless.
v.12             | KJV         | "they have wrought confusion" / ESV: "they have committed perversion" — Hebrew "tevel asu" — they have done confusion/ perversion. Same word as 18:23 (bestiality). Implies a violation of created order — mixing what God has distinguished.
v.14             | KJV         | "it is wickedness" / ESV: "it is depravity" — Hebrew "zimmah hi" — it is a scheme/plan/heinous sin. Implies calculated, deliberate wickedness — not a moment of weakness but a planned perversion.
v.17             | KJV         | "it is a wicked thing" / ESV: "it is a disgrace" — Hebrew "chesed hu" — here used in its negative sense: a shameful thing, a disgrace. (The same word "chesed" usually means lovingkindness — but in this rare usage it means shame/reproach. Context determines meaning.)
v.26             | KJV         | "have severed you from other people" / ESV: "have separated you from the peoples" — Hebrew "va-avdil etkhem min ha-ammim" — and I have divided/separated you from the peoples. "Avdil" = to divide, separate (same root as Genesis 1 — God "divided" light from darkness). God's separation of Israel mirrors His creative work of distinction.
v.27             | KJV         | "a familiar spirit, or that is a wizard" / ESV: "a medium or a necromancer" — Same terms as v.6. The chapter ends where it began — with the death penalty for occult practices. Bookend structure.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
His blood shall be upon him | A legal formula meaning the executed person bears responsibility for his own death. He brought it upon himself by his actions. The community is not guilty of murder in executing him — his blood is on his own head. | 
Molech worship    | Child sacrifice to the Ammonite/Canaanite deity Molech. Children were burned alive in the arms of a heated metal idol or passed through fire. The most extreme form of idolatry — offering what is most precious (children) to what is most evil (a demon). God's response is the most severe: death penalty + divine opposition ("I will set my face against"). | 
Set my face against | An expression of personal divine opposition. God Himself becomes the enemy of the one who commits these sins. Not merely legal punishment but personal hostility from the Almighty. Used for Molech worship (v.3), consulting mediums (v.6), and tolerating Molech worship (v.5). | 
Cut off" (Karat)  | Separation from the covenant community. May involve death by God's hand, excommunication, or extinction of family line. Less severe than execution by the community but still devastating — loss of covenant membership and its blessings. | 
Childlessness     | The penalty for certain sexual sins (vv.20-21). In ancient Israel, childlessness was the extinction of one's name and legacy. It meant no inheritance in the Promised Land, no continuation of the family line, no one to carry on your memory. A living death. | 
That ye should be mine | The purpose of separation. God does not separate His people merely to make them different but to make them His own possession. Holiness is not cold isolation but intimate belonging. Separation FROM the world is separation TO God. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Sanctify yourselves... I am the LORD which sanctify you" (vv.7-8). Both
   are true simultaneously. YOU must sanctify yourself (human
   responsibility). GOD sanctifies you (divine enablement). Holiness is not
   passive — you must pursue it. But it is not merely human effort — God
   empowers it. Work out your salvation, for God works in you (Philippians
   2:12-13).

2. "If the people of the land do any ways hide their eyes" (v.4). God holds
   the community responsible for tolerating evil. Looking away is not
   neutrality — it is complicity. When you see sin and say nothing, do
   nothing, you share in the guilt. Are you "hiding your eyes" from evil in
   your family, church, or community?

3. "They committed all these things, and therefore I abhorred them" (v.23).
   The Canaanites were not expelled for their ethnicity but for their
   behavior. God's judgment is moral, not racial. And the same standard
   applies to Israel — and to any nation. No people group is exempt from
   moral accountability before God.

4. "I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye
   should be mine" (v.26). The purpose of holiness is BELONGING. You are
   set apart not for isolation but for intimacy with God. "That ye should
   be mine" — this is the sweetest motivation for holiness. You are His
   treasured possession. Live like it.

5. "Such were some of you: but ye are washed" (1 Corinthians 6:11). The
   penalties in this chapter are severe — but the gospel offers cleansing
   even from these sins. No one is beyond redemption. The blood of Christ
   is sufficient for every sin listed here. The question is not "how bad is
   my sin?" but "how great is His grace?"

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Leviticus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/leviticus/

David Guzik — Leviticus 20 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/leviticus-20/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

21.ESV:
1. And the LORD said to Moses, “Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them, No one shall make himself unclean for the dead among his people,
2. except for his closest relatives, his mother, his father, his son, his daughter, his brother,
3. or his virgin sister (who is near to him because she has had no husband; for her he may make himself unclean).
4. He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself.
5. They shall not make bald patches on their heads, nor shave off the edges of their beards, nor make any cuts on their body.
6. They shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God. For they offer the LORD’s food offerings, the bread of their God; therefore they shall be holy.
7. They shall not marry a prostitute or a woman who has been defiled, neither shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God.
8. You shall sanctify him, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I, the LORD, who sanctify you, am holy.
9. And the daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by whoring, profanes her father; she shall be burned with fire.
10. “The priest who is chief among his brothers, on whose head the anointing oil is poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose nor tear his clothes.
11. He shall not go in to any dead bodies nor make himself unclean, even for his father or for his mother.
12. He shall not go out of the sanctuary, lest he profane the sanctuary of his God, for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is on him: I am the LORD.
13. And he shall take a wife in her virginity.
14. A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute, these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin of his own people,
15. that he may not profane his offspring among his people, for I am the LORD who sanctifies him.”
16. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
17. “Speak to Aaron, saying, None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach to offer the bread of his God.
18. For no one who has a blemish shall draw near, a man blind or lame, or one who has a mutilated face or a limb too long,
19. or a man who has an injured foot or an injured hand,
20. or a hunchback or a dwarf or a man with a defect in his sight or an itching disease or scabs or crushed testicles.
21. No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD’s food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God.
22. He may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy and of the holy things,
23. but he shall not go through the veil or approach the altar, because he has a blemish, that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am the LORD who sanctifies them.”
24. So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel.

21.ASV:
1. And Jehovah said unto Moses, Speak unto the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say unto them, There shall none defile himself for the dead among his people;
2. except for his kin, that is near unto him, for his mother, and for his father, and for his son, and for his daughter, and for his brother,
3. and for his sister a virgin, that is near unto him, that hath had no husband; for her may he defile himself.
4. He shall not defile himself, [being] a chief man among his people, to profane himself.
5. They shall not make baldness upon their head, neither shall they shave off the corner of their beard, nor make any cuttings in their flesh.
6. They shall be holy unto their God, and not profane the name of their God; for the offerings of Jehovah made by fire, the bread of their God, they do offer: therefore they shall be holy.
7. They shall not take a woman that is a harlot, or profane; neither shall they take a woman put away from her husband: for he is holy unto his God.
8. Thou shalt sanctify him therefore; for he offereth the bread of thy God: he shall be holy unto thee: for I Jehovah, who sanctify you, am holy.
9. And the daughter of any priest, if she profane herself by playing the harlot, she profaneth her father: she shall be burnt with fire.
10. And he that is the high priest among his brethren, upon whose head the anointing oil is poured, and that is consecrated to put on the garments, shall not let the hair of his head go loose, nor rend his clothes;
11. neither shall he go in to any dead body, nor defile himself for his father, or for his mother;
12. neither shall he go out of the sanctuary, nor profane the sanctuary of his God; for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him: I am Jehovah.
13. And he shall take a wife in her virginity.
14. A widow, or one divorced, or a profane woman, a harlot, these shall he not take: but a virgin of his own people shall he take to wife.
15. And he shall not profane his seed among his people: for I am Jehovah who sanctifieth him.
16. And Jehovah spake unto Moses, saying,
17. Speak unto Aaron, saying, Whosoever he be of thy seed throughout their generations that hath a blemish, let him not approach to offer the bread of his God.
18. For whatsoever man he be that hath a blemish, he shall not approach: a blind man, or a lame, or he that hath a flat nose, or anything superfluous,
19. or a man that is broken-footed, or broken-handed,
20. or crook-backed, or a dwarf, or that hath a blemish in his eye, or is scurvy, or scabbed, or hath his stones broken;
21. no man of the seed of Aaron the priest, that hath a blemish, shall come nigh to offer the offerings of Jehovah made by fire: he hath a blemish; he shall not come nigh to offer the bread of his God.
22. He shall eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy, and of the holy:
23. only he shall not go in unto the veil, nor come nigh unto the altar, because he hath a blemish; that he profane not my sanctuaries: for I am Jehovah who sanctifieth them.
24. So Moses spake unto Aaron, and to his sons, and unto all the children of Israel.
21.NET:
1. The Lord said to Moses, “Say to the priests, the sons of Aaron—say to them: ‘For a dead person no priest is to defile himself among his people,

3. and his virgin sister who is near to him, who has no husband—he may defile himself for her.

6. “‘They must be holy to their God, and they must not profane the name of their God, because they are the ones who present the Lord’s gifts, the food of their God. Therefore they must be holy.
7. They must not take a wife defiled by prostitution, nor are they to take a wife divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God.
8. You must sanctify him because he presents the food of your God. He must be holy to you because I, the Lord who sanctifies you all, am holy.
9. If a daughter of a priest profanes herself by engaging in prostitution, she is profaning her father. She must be burned to death.
10. “‘The high priest—who is greater than his brothers, and on whose head the anointing oil is poured, and who has been ordained to wear the priestly garments—must neither dishevel the hair of his head nor tear his garments.
11. He must not go where there is any dead person; he must not defile himself even for his father or for his mother.
12. He must not go out from the sanctuary and must not profane the sanctuary of his God, because the dedication of the anointing oil of his God is on him. I am the Lord.
13. He must take a wife who is a virgin.
14. He must not marry a widow, a divorced woman, or one profaned by prostitution; he may only take a virgin from his people as a wife,
15. so that he does not profane his children among his people, for I am the Lord who sanctifies him.’”
16. The Lord spoke to Moses:
17. “Tell Aaron, ‘No man from your descendants throughout their generations who has a physical flaw is to approach to present the food of his God.
18. Certainly no man who has a physical flaw is to approach: a blind man, or one who is lame, or one with a slit nose, or who has a limb too long,
19. or a man who has had a broken leg or arm,
20. or a hunchback, or a dwarf, or one with a spot in his eye, or a festering eruption, or a feverish rash, or a crushed testicle.
21. No man from the descendants of Aaron the priest who has a physical flaw may step forward to present the Lord’s gifts; he has a physical flaw, so he must not step forward to present the food of his God.
22. He may eat both the most holy and the holy food of his God,
23. but he must not go near the special curtain or step forward to the altar because he has a physical flaw. Thus he must not profane my holy places, for I am the Lord who sanctifies them.’”
24. So Moses spoke these things to Aaron, his sons, and all the Israelites.
21.WEB:
1. Yahweh said to Moses, “Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them, ‘A priest shall not defile himself for the dead among his people;
2. except for his relatives that are near to him: for his mother, for his father, for his son, for his daughter, for his brother,
3. and for his virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband; for her he may defile himself.
4. He shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people, to profane himself.
5. “‘They shall not shave their heads or shave off the corners of their beards or make any cuttings in their flesh.

7. “‘They shall not marry a woman who is a prostitute, or profane. They shall not marry a woman divorced from her husband; for he is holy to his God.

16. Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
17. “Say to Aaron, ‘None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a defect may approach to offer the bread of his God.
18. For whatever man he is that has a defect, he shall not draw near: a blind man, or a lame, or he who has a flat nose, or any deformity,
19. or a man who has an injured foot, or an injured hand,

22. He shall eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy, and of the holy.
23. He shall not come near to the veil, nor come near to the altar, because he has a defect; that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am Yahweh who sanctifies them.’”

21.notes:
Leviticus - Chapter 21
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God sets higher standards of holiness for priests — restrictions on mourning, marriage, and physical qualifications for those who serve at His altar.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Leviticus is the third book of the Pentateuch, written during
the wilderness period (approximately 1445-1405 BC). Hebrew title: "Vayikra"
— "And He called." God speaks from the newly completed Tabernacle, giving
Israel instructions for worship, sacrifice, and holy living.

Historical Context: Chapter 21 shifts focus from the holiness required of
ALL Israel (chapters 18-20) to the HIGHER holiness required of priests.
If all Israel must be holy, the priests must be holier still — and the
high priest holiest of all. The chapter establishes a graduated scale of
holiness: common Israelite → priest → high priest. Each level has stricter
requirements. The logic is proximity to God: the closer one serves to
God's presence, the higher the standard. Priests handle holy things, offer
God's "bread" (sacrifices), and mediate between God and the people. Their
lives must reflect the holiness of the One they serve. The chapter
addresses three areas: (1) restrictions on mourning and defilement by the
dead (vv.1-6, 10-12); (2) restrictions on marriage (vv.7-8, 13-15); and
(3) restrictions based on physical defects (vv.16-24). These regulations
are not about the personal worth of the priest but about the symbolic
integrity of the office. The priest represents God to the people — his
life and person must visibly reflect God's wholeness and holiness. This
chapter points forward to Christ, our perfect High Priest — without
blemish, without defilement, wholly consecrated to God (Hebrews 7:26).

Priests and Defilement by the Dead (vv.1-6): God speaks to Moses for "the
priests the sons of Aaron" (v.1). A priest "shall none be defiled for the
dead among his people" (v.1) — contact with a corpse makes one unclean
for seven days (Numbers 19:11). Exceptions: immediate family — mother,
father, son, daughter, brother (v.2), and a virgin sister who has no
husband (v.3). For these closest relatives, a priest may defile himself.
But he "shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people"
(v.4) — his position limits his mourning. Priests must not make bald
patches on their heads, shave the edges of their beards, or cut their
flesh (v.5) — these were pagan mourning customs. "They shall be holy unto
their God, and not profane the name of their God" (v.6). The reason: "the
offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the bread of their God, they do
offer: therefore they shall be holy" (v.6). Their function demands their
holiness.

Priestly Marriage Restrictions (vv.7-9): A priest shall not marry a
prostitute, a defiled woman, or a divorced woman (v.7). "For he is holy
unto his God" (v.7). The congregation must "sanctify him" — treat him as
holy (v.8). "He shall be holy unto thee: for I the LORD, which sanctify
you, am holy" (v.8). A priest's daughter who becomes a prostitute
"profaneth her father" — she is burned with fire (v.9). The priest's
family reflects on his office.

The High Priest — Highest Standard (vv.10-15): "He that is the high priest
among his brethren" — the one anointed and consecrated to wear the
garments (v.10). He shall NOT uncover his head (let hair hang loose) or
tear his clothes (v.10) — the normal signs of mourning are forbidden to
him entirely. He shall not go near ANY dead body — not even his father or
mother (v.11). He shall not leave the sanctuary (v.12). "The crown of the
anointing oil of his God is upon him" (v.12) — his consecration overrides
all other obligations. Marriage: he must take a virgin (v.13). Not a widow,
divorced woman, defiled woman, or prostitute — only "a virgin of his own
people" (v.14). The purpose: "that he may not profane his seed among his
people" (v.15). The high priest's family line must be pure. He represents
the highest standard of holiness in Israel.

Physical Blemishes and Priestly Service (vv.16-24): God speaks to Moses
for Aaron (v.16). No descendant of Aaron with a physical blemish may
"approach to offer the bread of his God" (v.17). The list: blind, lame,
mutilated face, limb too long (v.18), injured foot or hand (v.19),
hunchback, dwarf, eye defect, skin disease, scabs, crushed testicles
(v.20). No blemished priest may "come nigh to offer the offerings of the
LORD made by fire" (v.21). However — he MAY eat the priestly food, "both
of the most holy, and of the holy" (v.22). He retains his priestly
identity and provision — he simply cannot serve at the altar or enter
beyond the veil (v.23). "That he profane not my sanctuaries: for I the
LORD do sanctify them" (v.23). Moses communicates this to Aaron, his sons,
and all Israel (v.24).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Numbers     | 19      | 11-14              | Contact with the dead causes seven days of uncleanness.
v.1              | Ezekiel     | 44      | 25-27              | Regulations for priests and the dead in Ezekiel's temple vision.
v.5              | Leviticus   | 19      | 27-28              | The same prohibitions given to all Israel — but especially binding on priests.
v.5              | Deuteronomy | 14      | 1                  | "You shall not cut yourselves... for the dead." (ESV)
v.7              | Ezekiel     | 44      | 22                 | Priests in Ezekiel's vision: "they shall not marry a widow or a divorced woman." (ESV)
v.9              | Genesis     | 38      | 24                 | Judah orders Tamar burned for prostitution (she was his daughter-in-law, quasi-priestly family).
v.10             | Leviticus   | 10      | 6                  | After Nadab and Abihu's death, Aaron is told NOT to mourn: "Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes."
v.11             | Numbers     | 6       | 6-7                | The Nazirite vow includes the same restriction: no contact with the dead, even family.
v.12             | Exodus      | 29      | 7                  | The anointing of Aaron with oil.
v.13             | 2 Corinthians | 11      | 2                  | "I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ." (ESV)
v.14             | Hebrews     | 7       | 26                 | Christ our High Priest: "holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners." (ESV)
v.17             | Leviticus   | 22      | 20-25              | Blemished animals also cannot be offered — the same principle applies to both priest and sacrifice.
v.21             | Malachi     | 1       | 8                  | "When you offer blind animals in sacrifice, is that not evil?" (ESV)
v.22             | 1 Samuel    | 21      | 6                  | David eats the showbread — "the bread of God."
v.23             | Hebrews     | 7       | 26                 | Christ is the perfect, unblemished High Priest.
v.23             | 1 Peter     | 1       | 19                 | Christ: "a lamb without blemish or spot." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/leviticus-21/
  Guzik emphasizes the graduated holiness: all Israel is holy, priests are
  holier, the high priest is holiest. He notes that the physical blemish
  restrictions are not about personal worth but about symbolic
  representation — the priest's body must visibly represent God's
  wholeness. He connects this to Christ as the perfect High Priest.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Perfect Priest"
  "The priest must be without blemish — not because a lame man is less
  loved by God, but because the priest REPRESENTS God to the people. He is
  a living picture of the Holy One. And the picture must not be marred.
  Every physical requirement points forward to Christ — our High Priest
  who is 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made
  higher than the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26). No blemish in Him — no sin, no
  defect, no weakness, no failure. He is the perfect Priest who perfectly
  represents the perfect God. And because He is perfect, His offering is
  perfect, and His intercession is perfect, and His salvation is perfect.
  What Aaron could only picture, Christ IS."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "There shall none be defiled for the dead among his people" / ESV: "No one shall make himself unclean for the dead among his people" — Hebrew "lo yitamma b'ammav" — he shall not make himself unclean among his people. ESV's "make himself unclean" emphasizes the voluntary nature — the priest must actively avoid defilement.
v.4              | KJV         | "he shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people, to profane himself" / ESV: "He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself" — Hebrew "lo yitamma ba'al b'ammav l'hechallo." This verse is difficult. KJV reads "ba'al" as "chief/master" — his position prevents defilement. ESV reads "ba'al" as "husband" — he shall not defile himself for in-laws (by marriage). Both are possible readings of the Hebrew.
v.5              | KJV         | "They shall not make baldness upon their head" / ESV: "They shall not make bald patches on their heads" — Hebrew "lo yiqr'chu qorchah b'rosham." Shaving the head was a pagan mourning custom (also practiced in Egypt). Priests must not adopt pagan mourning rituals — their grief must be expressed within God's boundaries.
v.7              | KJV         | "a wife that is a whore, or profane" / ESV: "a prostitute or a woman who has been defiled" — Hebrew "zonah va-chalalah." "Zonah" = a prostitute/harlot. "Chalalah" = a profaned/defiled woman (one who has been sexually violated or who has lost her virginal status in a dishonorable way). KJV's "profane" is literal; ESV interprets as "defiled."
v.10             | KJV         | "shall not uncover his head, nor rend his clothes" / ESV: "shall not let the hair of his head hang loose nor tear his clothes" — Hebrew "et rosho lo yifra v'et b'gadav lo yifrom." "Yifra" = to let loose, unbind (the hair). KJV's "uncover" suggests removing a head covering; ESV's "let hang loose" refers to disheveling the hair as a sign of mourning. ESV is more accurate to the Hebrew.
v.12             | KJV         | "the crown of the anointing oil" / ESV: "the consecration of the anointing oil" — Hebrew "nezer shemen ha-mishchah." "Nezer" = crown, consecration, separation (same root as "Nazirite"). The anointing oil is his "crown" — his mark of consecration. It sets him apart as surely as a crown sets apart a king.
v.18             | KJV         | "a blind man, or a lame, or he that hath a flat nose, or any thing superfluous" / ESV: "a man blind or lame, or one who has a mutilated face or a limb too long" — Hebrew "iver o fisse'ach o charum o sarua." "Charum" = flat-nosed/mutilated face (KJV: "flat nose"; ESV: "mutilated face"). "Sarua" = extended/stretched (KJV: "superfluous" = extra; ESV: "limb too long"). Both translations attempt to render rare Hebrew terms for physical conditions.
v.20             | KJV         | "crookbackt, or a dwarf" / ESV: "a hunchback or a dwarf" — Hebrew "gibben o daq." "Gibben" = hunchbacked (KJV's "crookbackt" is archaic for the same). "Daq" = thin/small (a dwarf or emaciated person). These are rare terms, and exact identification is debated.
v.20             | KJV         | "hath his stones broken" / ESV: "crushed testicles" — Hebrew "m'ro'ach ashekh." KJV's "stones broken" is a euphemism; ESV is direct. A man with damaged reproductive organs cannot serve at the altar — the priest must represent wholeness and the capacity for life.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Bread of God" (Lechem Elohim) | A term for the sacrificial offerings, especially the food offerings burned on the altar. Not literal bread for God to eat, but the sacrifices presented as God's "food" — His portion. The priest who offers God's "bread" must be holy, just as the bread itself must be without defect. | 
Graduated Holiness | The principle that holiness increases with proximity to God. All Israel is holy (set apart from the nations). Priests are holier (set apart from Israel for service). The high priest is holiest (set apart even from other priests). The closer to God's presence, the stricter the requirements. | 
Anointing Oil (Shemen ha | Mishchah) — The sacred oil poured on the high priest's head at his consecration (Exodus 30:22-33). Made of myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia, and olive oil. It marks him as God's chosen representative. Called his "crown" (nezer) — his mark of authority and consecration. | 
Physical Blemish (Mum) | Any physical defect that disqualifies a priest from altar service. Not a statement about personal worth but about symbolic representation. The priest's body must visibly represent God's wholeness and perfection. A blemished priest may still eat holy food (v.22) — he retains his priestly identity. | 
Profane" (Chalal) | To make common, to defile, to treat as unholy. The opposite of "sanctify" (qadash). A priest profanes himself by violating his consecration — improper mourning, improper marriage, or improper service. To profane is to blur the line between holy and common. | 
Virgin (B'tulah)  | The high priest must marry a virgin — one who has not been "profaned" by prior sexual contact. This represents the purity and exclusivity of the priestly line. Points to Christ and His bride, the Church — "a chaste virgin" (2 Corinthians 11:2). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Greater privilege = greater responsibility. Priests have higher
   standards than common Israelites. The high priest has higher standards
   than regular priests. "Unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be
   much required" (Luke 12:48). If God has given you a position of
   spiritual leadership — pastor, teacher, parent, elder — you are held to
   a higher standard. Not because you are better, but because you represent
   God to others.

2. The high priest cannot mourn even for his parents (v.11). His
   consecration overrides even the deepest human bonds. Christ said: "He
   that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me" (Matthew
   10:37). Is there any relationship or obligation you place above your
   consecration to God?

3. A blemished priest may eat but not serve (v.22). He is not rejected — he
   is still provided for, still part of the priestly family. But he cannot
   represent God at the altar. This is not cruelty but integrity of
   representation. God's representative must visibly reflect God's
   wholeness. Christ fulfills this perfectly — He is the unblemished Priest
   (Hebrews 7:26).

4. "I the LORD do sanctify them" (v.23). God sanctifies both the priests
   and the sanctuaries. Holiness is ultimately God's work, not man's
   achievement. He sets apart, He consecrates, He makes holy. Your job is
   to cooperate with His sanctifying work — not to resist it or neglect it.

5. Every requirement points to Christ. No human priest could perfectly
   fulfill these standards — they all had blemishes of character if not of
   body. But Christ is "holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners"
   (Hebrews 7:26). He is the perfect Priest that every Aaronic priest could
   only foreshadow. Rest in His perfection, not your own.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Leviticus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/leviticus/

David Guzik — Leviticus 21 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/leviticus-21/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

22.ESV:
1. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
2. “Speak to Aaron and his sons so that they abstain from the holy things of the people of Israel, which they dedicate to me, so that they do not profane my holy name: I am the LORD.
3. Say to them, ‘If any one of all your offspring throughout your generations approaches the holy things that the people of Israel dedicate to the LORD, while he has an uncleanness, that person shall be cut off from my presence: I am the LORD.
4. None of the offspring of Aaron who has a leprous disease or a discharge may eat of the holy things until he is clean. Whoever touches anything that is unclean through contact with the dead or a man who has had an emission of semen,
5. and whoever touches a swarming thing by which he may be made unclean or a person from whom he may take uncleanness, whatever his uncleanness may be—
6. the person who touches such a thing shall be unclean until the evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.
7. When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, because they are his food.
8. He shall not eat what dies of itself or is torn by beasts, and so make himself unclean by it: I am the LORD.’
9. They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it: I am the LORD who sanctifies them.
10. “A lay person shall not eat of a holy thing; no foreign guest of the priest or hired worker shall eat of a holy thing,
11. but if a priest buys a slave as his property for money, the slave may eat of it, and anyone born in his house may eat of his food.
12. If a priest’s daughter marries a layman, she shall not eat of the contribution of the holy things.
13. But if a priest’s daughter is widowed or divorced and has no child and returns to her father’s house, as in her youth, she may eat of her father’s food; yet no lay person shall eat of it.
14. And if anyone eats of a holy thing unintentionally, he shall add the fifth of its value to it and give the holy thing to the priest.
15. They shall not profane the holy things of the people of Israel, which they contribute to the LORD,
16. and so cause them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things: for I am the LORD who sanctifies them.”
17. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
18. “Speak to Aaron and his sons and all the people of Israel and say to them, When any one of the house of Israel or of the sojourners in Israel presents a burnt offering as his offering, for any of their vows or freewill offerings that they offer to the LORD,
19. if it is to be accepted for you it shall be a male without blemish, of the bulls or the sheep or the goats.
20. You shall not offer anything that has a blemish, for it will not be acceptable for you.
21. And when anyone offers a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering from the herd or from the flock, to be accepted it must be perfect; there shall be no blemish in it.
22. Animals blind or disabled or mutilated or having a discharge or an itch or scabs you shall not offer to the LORD or give them to the LORD as a food offering on the altar.
23. You may present a bull or a lamb that has a part too long or too short for a freewill offering, but for a vow offering it cannot be accepted.
24. Any animal that has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut you shall not offer to the LORD; you shall not do it within your land,
25. neither shall you offer as the bread of your God any such animals gotten from a foreigner. Since there is a blemish in them, because of their mutilation, they will not be accepted for you.”
26. And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
27. “When an ox or sheep or goat is born, it shall remain seven days with its mother, and from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to the LORD.
28. But you shall not kill an ox or a sheep and her young in one day.
29. And when you sacrifice a sacrifice of thanksgiving to the LORD, you shall sacrifice it so that you may be accepted.
30. It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning: I am the LORD.
31. “So you shall keep my commandments and do them: I am the LORD.
32. And you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the people of Israel. I am the LORD who sanctifies you,
33. who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God: I am the LORD.”

9. They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it, and die therein, if they profane it: I am Jehovah who sanctifieth them.
10. There shall no stranger eat of the holy thing: a sojourner of the priest`s, or a hired servant, shall not eat of the holy thing.
11. But if a priest buy any soul, the purchase of his money, he shall eat of it; and such as are born in his house, they shall eat of his bread.
12. And if a priest`s daughter be married unto a stranger, she shall not eat of the heave-offering of the holy things.
13. But if a priest`s daughter be a widow, or divorced, and have no child, and be returned unto her father`s house, as in her youth, she shall eat of her father`s bread: but there shall no stranger eat thereof.
14. And if a man eat of the holy thing unwittingly, then he shall put the fifth part thereof unto it, and shall give unto the priest the holy thing.
15. And they shall not profane the holy things of the children of Israel, which they offer unto Jehovah,
16. and [so] cause them to bear the iniquity that bringeth guilt, when they eat their holy things: for I am Jehovah who sanctifieth them.
17. And Jehovah spake unto Moses, saying,
18. Speak unto Aaron, and to his sons, and unto all the children of Israel, and say unto them, Whosoever he be of the house of Israel, or of the sojourners in Israel, that offereth his oblation, whether it be any of their vows, or any of their freewill-offerings, which they offer unto Jehovah for a burnt-offering;
19. that ye may be accepted, [ye shall offer] a male without blemish, of the bullocks, of the sheep, or of the goats.
20. But whatsoever hath a blemish, that shall ye not offer: for it shall not be acceptable for you.
21. And whosoever offereth a sacrifice of peace-offerings unto Jehovah to accomplish a vow, or for a freewill-offering, of the herd or of the flock, it shall be perfect to be accepted; there shall be no blemish therein.
22. Blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a wen, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these unto Jehovah, nor make an offering by fire of them upon the altar unto Jehovah.
23. Either a bullock or a lamb that hath anything superfluous or lacking in his parts, that mayest thou offer for a freewill-offering; but for a vow it shall not be accepted.
24. That which hath its stones bruised, or crushed, or broken, or cut, ye shall not offer unto Jehovah; neither shall ye do [thus] in your land.
25. Neither from the hand of a foreigner shall ye offer the bread of your God of any of these; because their corruption is in them, there is a blemish in them: they shall not be accepted for you.
26. And Jehovah spake unto Moses, saying,
27. When a bullock, or a sheep, or a goat, is brought forth, then it shall be seven days under the dam; and from the eighth day and thenceforth it shall be accepted for the oblation of an offering made by fire unto Jehovah.
28. And whether it be cow or ewe, ye shall not kill it and its young both in one day.
29. And when ye sacrifice a sacrifice of thanksgiving unto Jehovah, ye shall sacrifice it that ye may be accepted.
30. On the same day it shall be eaten; ye shall leave none of it until the morning: I am Jehovah.
31. Therefore shall ye keep my commandments, and do them: I am Jehovah.
32. And ye shall not profane my holy name; but I will be hallowed among the children of Israel: I am Jehovah who halloweth you,
33. who brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: I am Jehovah.
22.NET:

7. When the sun goes down he will be clean, and afterward he may eat from the holy offerings, because they are his food.
8. He must not eat an animal that has died of natural causes or an animal torn by beasts and thus become unclean by it. I am the Lord.
9. They must keep my charge so that they do not incur sin on account of it and therefore die because they profane it. I am the Lord who sanctifies them.
10. “‘No lay person may eat anything holy. Neither a priest’s lodger nor a hired laborer may eat anything holy,
11. but if a priest buys a person with his own money, that person may eat the holy offerings, and those born in the priest’s own house may eat his food.
12. If a priest’s daughter marries a lay person, she may not eat the holy contribution offerings,
13. but if a priest’s daughter is a widow or divorced, and she has no children so that she returns to live in her father’s house as in her youth, she may eat from her father’s food, but no lay person may eat it.

15. They must not profane the holy offerings which the Israelites contribute to the Lord,

17. The Lord spoke to Moses:
18. “Speak to Aaron, his sons, and all the Israelites and tell them, ‘When any man from the house of Israel or from the resident foreigners in Israel presents his offering for any of the votive or freewill offerings, which they present to the Lord as a burnt offering,
19. if it is to be acceptable for your benefit, it must be a flawless male from the cattle, sheep, or goats.
20. You must not present anything that has a flaw, because it will not be acceptable for your benefit.
